Senate President Gatchalian noted that since convening on May 18, 2026, the Court had 
issued summons and conducted pre-trial proceedings, all of which were made part of the record. 
He also stated that Resolution No. 39, as amended, shall continue to govern the impeachment 
proceedings.

blocks.normalized_text: sa araw na ito binubuksan natin ang pagdinig sa kasong impeachment laban sa pangalawang pangulo ng republika ng pilipinas ang kagalang-galang na sara zimmerman duterte alinsunod sa ating saligang batas
blocks.raw_text: Sa araw na ito, binubuksan natin ang pagdinig sa kasong impeachment laban sa Pangalawang Pangulo ng Republika ng Pilipinas, ang kagalang-galang na Sara Zimmerman Duterte, alinsunod sa ating Saligang Batas.
blocks.section_title: STATEMENT OF SENATE PRESIDENT WIN GATCHALIAN
blocks.source_text:
Sa araw na ito, binubuksan natin ang pagdinig sa kasong impeachment laban sa 
Pangalawang Pangulo ng Republika ng Pilipinas, ang kagalang-galang na Sara Zimmerman 
Duterte, alinsunod sa ating Saligang Batas.

blocks.normalized_text: sa pagtupad natin sa tungkuling ito malinaw ang panata na ating sinumpaan to do impartial justice according to the constitution and the laws of the philippines ibig sabihin nito ang bawat pasya natin ay dapat nakabatay lamang sa saligang batas sa ating mga batas sa mga alituntunin ng hukumang ito at sa mga ebidensyang ihaharap na walang kinikilingan at walang pinapanigan
blocks.raw_text: Sa pagtupad natin sa tungkuling ito, malinaw ang panata na ating sinumpaan: “to do impartial justice according to the Constitution and the laws of the Philippines.” Ibig sabihin nito, ang bawat pasya natin ay dapat nakabatay lamang sa Saligang Batas, sa ating mga batas, sa mga alituntunin ng Hukumang ito, at sa mga ebidensyang ihaharap na walang kinikilingan at walang pinapanigan.
blocks.section_title: STATEMENT OF SENATE PRESIDENT WIN GATCHALIAN
blocks.source_text:
Sa pagtupad natin sa tungkuling ito, malinaw ang panata na ating sinumpaan: “to do 
impartial justice according to the Constitution and the laws of the Philippines.” Ibig sabihin nito, 
ang bawat pasya natin ay dapat nakabatay lamang sa Saligang Batas, sa ating mga batas, sa 
mga alituntunin ng Hukumang ito, at sa mga ebidensyang ihaharap na walang kinikilingan at 
walang pinapanigan.

blocks.normalized_text: impeachment is the constitutional mechanism that holds the highest public officials of the republic accountable to the people we cannot achieve accountability nor can we faithfully serve justice without the truth
blocks.raw_text: Impeachment is the constitutional mechanism that holds the highest public officials of the Republic accountable to the people. We cannot achieve accountability nor can we faithfully serve justice without the truth.
blocks.section_title: STATEMENT OF SENATE PRESIDENT WIN GATCHALIAN
blocks.source_text:
Impeachment is the constitutional mechanism that holds the highest public officials of the 
Republic accountable to the people. We cannot achieve accountability nor can we faithfully serve 
justice without the truth.

blocks.raw_text: Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) raised a point of order, stating that under the Constitution, the Presiding Officer of the Impeachment Court is the Senate President. He stated that while Article XI, Section 3(6) of the 1987 Constitution does not explicitly say as who shall preside over the impeachment trials of public officials other than the President of the Philippines, the record of the proceedings of the 1986 Constitutional Commission, particularly Session No. 41, dated July 27, 1986, shows the following: “xxx THE PRESIDING OFFICER (Mr. Trenas): Commissioner Davide is recognized.
blocks.section_title: POINT OF ORDER OF SENATOR CAYETANO (A)
blocks.source_text:
Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) raised a point of order, stating that under the Constitution, 
the Presiding Officer of the Impeachment Court is the Senate President. He stated that while 
Article XI, Section 3(6) of the 1987 Constitution does not explicitly say as who shall preside over 
the impeachment trials of public officials other than the President of the Philippines, the record of 
the proceedings of the 1986 Constitutional Commission, particularly Session No. 41, dated July 27, 
1986, shows the following:
“xxx THE PRESIDING OFFICER (Mr. Trenas): Commissioner Davide is recognized.

blocks.section_title: POINT OF ORDER OF SENATOR CAYETANO (A)

Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) recalled that when he was still the Senate President, there was 
a procedural dispute over the proposed amendment of Senator Marcoleta to the Rules of the 
Senate that would allow senators to participate in the plenary sessions remotely. He noted, 
however, that the minority then insisted strict compliance with the Rules, even though the proposal 
had been pending for more than one day and had been referred to the Committee on Rules. He 
also questioned the validity of the June 3, 2026 session, arguing that it lacked the required quorum 
- a question that is pending before the Supreme Court. He further asserted, assuming for the sake 
of argument that twelve senators constituted a quorum, that the Resolution amending the 
impeachment Rules should have been presented a day before. Any amendment thereto must 
likewise be adopted by the Impeachment Court after proper deliberation.

blocks.section_title: POINT OF ORDER OF SENATOR CAYETANO (A)
blocks.source_text:
Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) argued that the issue as to who should preside over the 
impeachment trial is a threshold matter that must first be resolved to avoid legal issues that could 
render the entire proceedings null and void. He stressed his objection to the election of another 
Presiding Officer, noting that Senate President Gatchalian had already expressed his willingness to 
preside. He explained that the issue on the presiding officer, like the issue on the required 2/3 vote 
to convict, is a threshold issue - a preliminary question or condition that must be resolved before 
proceeding to the merits of the case, absent which, further action is halted.

blocks.raw_text: Senate President Gatchalian stated that the amendment to Rule II of the Rules of Procedure on Impeachment Trials was adopted pursuant to Article XI, Section 3(8) of the Constitution, which explicitly provides that “Congress to promulgate its own impeachment rules to effectively carry out the purposes of this Section.” He recalled that the amendment introduced by Senator Zubiri through Senate Resolution No. 430 on June 3, 2026, was duly approved and adopted with no objection from any Member. blocks.source_text:
Senate President Gatchalian stated that the amendment to Rule II of the Rules of Procedure 
on Impeachment Trials was adopted pursuant to Article XI, Section 3(8) of the Constitution, which 
explicitly provides that “Congress to promulgate its own impeachment rules to effectively carry out 
the purposes of this Section.” He recalled that the amendment introduced by Senator Zubiri through 
Senate Resolution No. 430 on June 3, 2026, was duly approved and adopted with no objection from 
any Member. It was later renumbered as Resolution No. 48 and published in the Philippine Daily 
Inquirer and Malaya on June 9, 2026. He ruled that any challenge or discussion on the matter 
should be taken up by the Senate in plenary session as a legislative body, consistent with the 
adoption of the Rules on impeachment proceedings against former President Estrada in 2000, and 
former Chief Justice Corona in 2011.

Senator-Judge Pangilinan stated that the Constitution expressly requires the Chief Justice to 
preside only when the President of the Philippines is the respondent in an impeachment trial, 
stressing that nowhere in the provisions of the Constitution does it say that the Senate President 
must preside when the Vice President or any other impeachable officer is on trial. He maintained 
that it is precisely because of this silence that Congress is allowed to exercise its rule-making 
authority by prescribing its own rules, which is what the Senate did on June 3. He also noted that 
the amendments to the Rules on Procedure on Impeachment Trials had been ratified by the 
Senate during its approval of the Journal in special session on June 17, 2026, giving rise to the 
presumption of regularity.

Senator-Judge Cayetano (P) cited Lambino v. Commission on Elections (G.R. No. 174153), 
in which the Supreme Court relied on the deliberations of the Constitutional Commission in 
determining the nature of the people’s initiative. She explained that the Court gave effect to the 
framers’ explicit intent, as reflected in the plenary debates, in order to prevent deceptive or 
misleading petition processes, and to require compliance with the full-text requirement. She likewise 
cited Calderon v. Carale (G.R. No. 91636), wherein the Supreme Court examined the historical 
and textual context of Article VII, Section 16 of the 1987 Constitution and held that the framers 
intended to exclude presidential appointments from confirmation by the Commission on Appointments 
except those expressly specified in the Constitution. Applying the same principle to the present 
matter, she argued that the framers clearly intended that the Senate President should preside over 
the impeachment proceedings.

to construe the Constitution fi-om what appears upon its face.” The Court further held that the

the constitutional text—not to read into it qualifications or restrictions that it does not contain.

blocks.raw_text: This reading is reinforced by Article XI, Section 3(8), which expressly directs Congress to promulgate its Rules on Impeachment to effectively carry out the constitutional process. This provision is itself an express grant of constitutional authority. It recognizes that while the Constitution establishes the framework for impeachment, it entrusts Congress, in this instance, the Senate, with the responsibility of adopting the procedural rules necessary for the effective conduct of impeachment proceedings. Further, Article VI, Section 16(3) likewise recognizes that each House may determine the rules of its proceedings, a constitutional prerogative consistently respected by the Supreme Court as part of the Senate's institutional autonomy.
blocks.source_text:
This reading is reinforced by Article XI, Section 3(8), which expressly directs Congress to 
promulgate its Rules on Impeachment to effectively carry out the constitutional process. This 
provision is itself an express grant of constitutional authority. It recognizes that while the 
Constitution establishes the framework for impeachment, it entrusts Congress, in this instance, the 
Senate, with the responsibility of adopting the procedural rules necessary for the effective conduct 
of impeachment proceedings. Further, Article VI, Section 16(3) likewise recognizes that each House 
may determine the rules of its proceedings, a constitutional prerogative consistently respected 
by the Supreme Court as part of the Senate's institutional autonomy.

blocks.raw_text: The constitutional text is already clear, and if any confirmation is needed, the deliberations of the 1986 Constitutional Commission show that Commissioner Florenz Regalado proposed the inclusion of what is now Article XI, Section 3(8) precisely to ensure that Congress would have the authority to promulgate the procedural rules necessary to effectively carry out impeachment proceedings. blocks.source_text:
The constitutional text is already clear, and if any confirmation is needed, the deliberations 
of the 1986 Constitutional Commission show that Commissioner Florenz Regalado proposed the 
inclusion of what is now Article XI, Section 3(8) precisely to ensure that Congress would have 
the authority to promulgate the procedural rules necessary to effectively carry out impeachment 
proceedings. The proposal was accepted by the Commission without objection, confirming that 
the Constitution deliberately vested Congress with broad authority to establish the procedural 
rules governing impeachment proceedings, including procedural matters not expressly addressed 
by the constitutional text.

The amendment of Rule II likewise falls within the settled principle that parties acquire no 
vested right in procedural rules. In Tan v. Court of Appeals, G.R. No. 136368, the Supreme Court 
held that there are no vested rights in rules of procedure, which may be amended or repealed by 
the rule-making authority and generally apply to pending proceedings insofar as they regulate the 
mode of enforcing rights rather than the rights themselves. The amendment to Rule II did not create, 
diminish, or impair any substantive constitutional right. It merely prescribed the procedure by which 
the Senate, exercising its authority to promulgate rules for impeachment trials, may determine who 
shall preside in impeachment cases not involving the President.

blocks.normalized_text: while the deliberations of the constitutional commission may serve as an aid in resolving genuine ambiguity they cannot insert into the constitution a requirement that does not appear in its final text as the supreme court explained in francisco vs house of representatives g r no 160261 10 november 2003 the language of the constitution itself compels acceptance and negates the power of the courts to alter it based on the fundamental premise that the framers and the people who ratified the constitution meant what they said the supreme court further emphasized that the proceedings of the constitutional commission are powerless to vary the terms of the constitution when the meaning is clear
blocks.raw_text: While the deliberations of the Constitutional Commission may serve as an aid in resolving genuine ambiguity, they cannot insert into the Constitution a requirement that does not appear in its final text. As the Supreme Court explained in Francisco vs. House of Representatives, G.R. No. 160261, 10 November 2003, the language of the Constitution itself “compels acceptance and negates the power of the courts to alter it,” based on the fundamental premise that the framers and the people who ratified the Constitution meant what they said. The Supreme Court further emphasized that the proceedings of the Constitutional Commission are “powerless to vary the terms of the Constitution when the meaning is clear.”
blocks.source_text:
While the deliberations of the Constitutional Commission may serve as an aid in resolving 
genuine ambiguity, they cannot insert into the Constitution a requirement that does not appear 
in its final text. As the Supreme Court explained in Francisco vs. House of Representatives,
G.R. No. 160261, 10 November 2003, the language of the Constitution itself “compels acceptance 
and negates the power of the courts to alter it,” based on the fundamental premise that the framers 
and the people who ratified the Constitution meant what they said. The Supreme Court further 
emphasized that the proceedings of the Constitutional Commission are “powerless to vary the terms 
of the Constitution when the meaning is clear.”

Pangalawa, siya ang kauna-unaHang Bise Presidente na HaHarap sa paglilitis ng isang impeachment court, at pangalawang opisyal lamang na Halal ng sambayanan na sasailalim sa prosesong ito. Pangatlo, siya ay Hinalal nang may pinakamalaking mandatong pinagkaloob ng sambayanang Pilipino sa sinumang kandidato sa ating kasaysayan—maHigit 32 milyon o 61% ng kabuuang boto para sa Ikalawang Pangulo. Pang-apat, ang Articles of Impeachment laban sa kanya ay in-endorso ng 277 na miyembro na kumakatawan sa ating mga mamamayan mula sa iba't ibang distrito at sektor, at ng ating bansa, na katumbas ng maHigit 80% ng kabuuang miyembro ng Camara de Representantes.

Baku? Una, si Bise Presidente Sara Duterte ang kauna-unaHang opisyal na dalawang beses 
na na-impeach ng Mababang Kapulungan. Pangalawa, siya ang kauna-unaHang Bise Presidente 
na HaHarap sa paglilitis ng isang impeachment court, at pangalawang opisyal lamang na 
Halal ng sambayanan na sasailalim sa prosesong ito. Pangatlo, siya ay Hinalal nang may 
pinakamalaking mandatong pinagkaloob ng sambayanang Pilipino sa sinumang kandidato sa 
ating kasaysayan—maHigit 32 milyon o 61% ng kabuuang boto para sa Ikalawang Pangulo. 
Pang-apat, ang Articles of Impeachment laban sa kanya ay in-endorso ng 277 na miyembro na 
kumakatawan sa ating mga mamamayan mula sa iba't ibang distrito at sektor, at ng ating bansa, 
na katumbas ng maHigit 80% ng kabuuang miyembro ng Camara de Representantes.

blocks.raw_text: Terminating an elected mandate before the end of its term is among the most drastic acts our Constitutional democracy can undertake. blocks.source_text:
Terminating an elected mandate before the end of its term is among the most drastic acts our 
Constitutional democracy can undertake. This is why the trial must be guided by prudence, 
tempered by caution, and marked with unquestionable impartiality. We must avoid even the 
appearance of impropriety and observe the cold neutrality of an impartial judge at all times.

This now brings me to the threshold questions that will guide the conduct of this trial and 
the rulings of the Chair. First, what is the number of votes needed to convict the respondent? 
Second, what is the quantum of proof required to sustain a judgment of conviction or acquittal 
in these proceedings? Third, how strictly or liberally will this court apply the Rules in resolving 
questions of procedure and evidence?

This is the prevailing law today, and whether we agree with it or not, we must abide by it. 
However, those who disagree are not without recourse, because this is without prejudice to the 
right of any party who holds a different view to seek judicial relief. And should the Supreme 
Court—which possesses the sole authority to interpret the Constitution by virtue of its power of 
judicial review—render a different ruling on this question other than what the Chair has stated, this 
court and this Representation shall faithfully abide by that ruling of the Supreme Court.

blocks.raw_text: On the question of quantum of proof, given that impeachment is a sui generis constitutional proceeding, the standards of proof that govern administrative, civil, and criminal proceedings do not apply. However, this does not mean that senator-judges shall rely solely on their conscience in deciding this case. No less than the Supreme Court also said that “Decisions regarding whether to acquit or convict must be based upon clear charges supported by sufficiently clear and convincing evidence.”
blocks.source_text:
On the question of quantum of proof, given that impeachment is a sui generis constitutional 
proceeding, the standards of proof that govern administrative, civil, and criminal proceedings do 
not apply. However, this does not mean that senator-judges shall rely solely on their conscience 
in deciding this case. No less than the Supreme Court also said that “Decisions regarding whether 
to acquit or convict must be based upon clear charges supported by sufficiently clear and 
convincing evidence.”

evidence, it is a better policy to rule in favor of admission and let the senator-judges decide whether 
to give it little or no weight when deciding upon the case in the final outcome.

blocks.raw_text: Accordingly, consistent with the demands of due process, fair play, and the orderly administration of justice, the Chair will construe the rules liberally so that, number one, the prosecutors may be able to adequately present their case; that the respondent may be able to rigorously test it; number three, that the senator-judges may be able to weigh the evidence for themselves; and fourth, so that the Filipino people may hear the whole and impartial truth regarding the allegations against the Vice President.

Accordingly, consistent with the demands of due process, fair play, and the orderly 
administration of justice, the Chair will construe the rules liberally so that, number one, the 
prosecutors may be able to adequately present their case; that the respondent may be able to 
rigorously test it; number three, that the senator-judges may be able to weigh the evidence for 
themselves; and fourth, so that the Filipino people may hear the whole and impartial truth regarding 
the allegations against the Vice President.

blocks.raw_text: In support of the motion, she cited Rules 7, 9, and 21 of the Rules of Procedure on Impeachment Trials, arguing that Rule 7 contemplates the entry of a plea where the respondent or counsel fails to appear or file an Answer; that Rule 9 requires the respondent to be called upon to answer the Articles of Impeachment and the appearance of counsel to be entered into the record; and that Rule 21 provides that all Articles of Impeachment shall first be tried before the Senator-Judges vote on whether the impeachment is sustained, thereby indicating that each Article is to be considered separately.
blocks.source_text:
In support of the motion, she cited Rules 7, 9, and 21 of the Rules of Procedure on 
Impeachment Trials, arguing that Rule 7 contemplates the entry of a plea where the respondent 
or counsel fails to appear or file an Answer; that Rule 9 requires the respondent to be called upon 
to answer the Articles of Impeachment and the appearance of counsel to be entered into the 
record; and that Rule 21 provides that all Articles of Impeachment shall first be tried before the 
Senator-Judges vote on whether the impeachment is sustained, thereby indicating that each Article 
is to be considered separately.

blocks.normalized_text: she further argued that reading each article into the record and requiring a corresponding plea would ensure compliance with the constitution and the rules uphold the respondent s right to due process by informing her of the nature of each accusation before the entry of a plea and recognize that the four articles are based on distinct factual allegations and grounds such that different pleas may be entered with respect to each article
blocks.raw_text: She further argued that reading each Article into the record and requiring a corresponding plea would ensure compliance with the Constitution and the Rules, uphold the respondent’s right to due process by informing her of the nature of each accusation before the entry of a plea, and recognize that the four Articles are based on distinct factual allegations and grounds, such that different pleas may be entered with respect to each Article.
blocks.source_text:

process by informing her of the nature of each accusation before the entry of a plea, and recognize 
that the four Articles are based on distinct factual allegations and grounds, such that different pleas 
may be entered with respect to each Article.

With respect to Rule 7, Attorney Sison contended that the provision applies only 
when the respondent fails to appear or file an Answer, in which case a plea of “Not Guilty” 
is entered. She maintained that the rule neither requires nor contemplates an arraignment 
and was inapplicable because the respondent had appeared through counsel and had timely 
filed her Answer.

As to Rule 9, she argued that it merely requires that the respondent be called upon to answer 
the Articles of Impeachment and that the appearance of the respondent or counsel be entered into 
the record. She asserted that these requirements had already been satisfied through the respondent’s 
appearance by counsel and that nothing in the Rules requires the reading in open court of the 23- 
page Articles of Impeachment.

The framers of the Constitution understood that there may come a day when extraordinary 
power would be entrusted to extraordinary office. They also understood that there may come a day 
when trust would be broken. And that is why they gave the House of Representatives the power 
to impeach, and the Senate the duty to judge—not to punish political opponents, not to settle 
political scores, but to protect the Republic itself
That is why the Constitution declares: “Public office is a public trust.” Ang kapangyarihan 
ay hindi pag-aari ng sinumang opisyal; hiram lamang ito sa taumbayan, at ito ang dahilan kung 
bakit naririto tayo ngayon.

Public office is merely borrowed, and every borrowed power carries with it the obligation to 
account for its use. If a barangay treasurer must account for public funds, then so must the Vice 
President. If an ordinary public servant can be investigated, then so can the highest officials of 
the government. If an ordinary citizen is expected to obey the rules, then surely those who govern 
them must obey them first. Otherwise, what lessons do we teach our children? That there is a law 
for the powerful and another for everyone else? That accountability exists only for the weak? That 
public office grants immunity against accountability? Your Honors, this is not the republic 
envisioned by the Constitution. This is not the republic deserved by the Filipino people. Therefore, 
the prosecution asks only this: look at the evidence, listen to the witnesses, examine the records, 
and follow the evidence wherever it leads. Judge this case, not by politics, not by popularity, not 
by fear, and not by loyalty. Judge this case by the Constitution. Judge this case by the evidence.

Indeed, the people have a right to demand accountability from their leaders. What we have 
yet to hear from the prosecution, however, is this: when the Constitution declared, under Section 
1, Article XI, that public office is a public trust, and that “public officers and employees must at 
all times be accountable to the people, serve them with utmost responsibility, integrity, loyalty, and 
efficiency, act with patriotism and justice, and lead modest lives,’’ the Constitution does not only 
speak to the Vice President. It also demands the same standards from the prosecutors. Being public 
officers themselves, the public prosecutors and the Members of the House of Representatives are 
also held to the same constitutional standards.

In a July 25, 2025 Decision, the Supreme Court unanimously declared in Duterte v. House of 
Representatives, G.R. Nos. 278353 and 278359, that the impeachment proceedings in the House of 
Representatives were tainted with grave abuse of discretion, rendering the Articles of Impeachment 
that the House transmitted last year to the Senate as void ab initio.

blocks.raw_text: Early this year, the Supreme Court unanimously denied, with finality, the House of Representatives’ Motion for Reconsideration of that Decision, with a reminder that while impeachment is a powerful democratic process to call out corruption and grave abuse, impeachment can be abused, and that impeachment should never be abused to maintain the hegemonic dominance of greed by shaming those who occupy high government positions into preventing them from doing what they were sworn to do. But perhaps the most enduring reminder of the Supreme Court that we must not forget in the entire course of this trial is that, in the words of the Court—the rule of law that does justice is our lodestar—and that justice includes accountability. Justice includes fairness. Without fairness, there is abuse. This fairness is what our democracy is all about.
blocks.source_text:
Early this year, the Supreme Court unanimously denied, with finality, the House of 
Representatives’ Motion for Reconsideration of that Decision, with a reminder that while 
impeachment is a powerful democratic process to call out corruption and grave abuse, impeachment 
can be abused, and that impeachment should never be abused to maintain the hegemonic 
dominance of greed by shaming those who occupy high government positions into preventing 
them from doing what they were sworn to do. But perhaps the most enduring reminder of the 
Supreme Court that we must not forget in the entire course of this trial is that, in the words of the 
Court—the rule of law that does justice is our lodestar—and that justice includes accountability. 
Justice includes fairness. Without fairness, there is abuse. This fairness is what our democracy is 
all about.

Over the course of the impeachment trial, the prosecution will attempt to convince this Court— 
and the court of public opinion—that the allegations in the Articles of Impeachment are supported 
by materials they obtained during their mini-trial, and that evidence exists to sustain a judgment 
of conviction. They will claim that the notices of suspension and disallowance issued by the COA 
and the COA decision denying the Vice President’s appeal of that disallowance support their 
accusations, with the release of that decision by the COA conveniently timed and coinciding with 
the committee hearings. They will claim that the certifications of the PSA confirm their allegation 
that recipients of the confidential funds do not exist, even though they listed one Mary Grace 
Piattos in their Pre-Trial Brief as among their witnesses, or that the liquidation of the confidential 
funds in a span of 11 days was allegedly swift as to invite suspicion. The prosecution will assert 
that the supposed NBI findings of similar signatures across a number of acknowledgment receipts 
submitted to the COA are supportive of their conclusion that the disbursements of the confidential 
funds were irregular.

blocks.raw_text: What the prosecution avoided informing the public so far are these: That it was the then- Secretary of the Department of Budget and Management, Amenah Pangandaman, who issued a memorandum for the President, through Executive Secretary Lucas Bersamin on October 18, 2022, recommending the approval of the Office of the Vice President’s request for confidential funds; that the Office of the President did, in fact, approve that recommendation of the DBM through a memorandum from the Executive Secretary dated November 28, 2022, and signed by the former Executive Secretary Lucas Bersamin by order of the President; that on December 13, 2022, the DBM issued a Special Allotment Order Number - SARO-BMB-C-22-0012004, signed by former Secretary Amenah Pangandaman, to cover the request for financial assistance, subsidy, and confidential funds of the OVP; that notwithstanding the impending end of the fiscal year, the confidential funds were nonetheless released to the OVP on December 20, 2022, under this documented approval process; that the COA decision is far from being final; that there is a pending motion for reconsideration of that decision; that, assuming argMent/o, the COA denies that MR today, or while this trial is ongoing, as may plausibly happen under the circumstances, the respondent accused still has legal remedies available imder the law and rules, and can file a petition to question the validity of any forthcoming resolution of the COA on the MR under Rule 64, via Petition for Certiorari under Rule 65 of the Rules of Court; and, more importantly, that nothing in the Notice of Disallowance or COA decision states any findings of supposed misuse of confidential funds, as confirmed by Atty. Gloria Camora when she was asked on this point during the committee hearing on April 14, 2026.
blocks.source_text:
What the prosecution avoided informing the public so far are these: That it was the then- 
Secretary of the Department of Budget and Management, Amenah Pangandaman, who issued a 
memorandum for the President, through Executive Secretary Lucas Bersamin on October 18, 2022, 
recommending the approval of the Office of the Vice President’s request for confidential funds; that 
the Office of the President did, in fact, approve that recommendation of the DBM through a 
memorandum from the Executive Secretary dated November 28, 2022, and signed by the former 
Executive Secretary Lucas Bersamin by order of the President; that on December 13, 2022, the DBM 
issued a Special Allotment Order Number - SARO-BMB-C-22-0012004, signed by former Secretary 
Amenah Pangandaman, to cover the request for financial assistance, subsidy, and confidential 
funds of the OVP; that notwithstanding the impending end of the fiscal year, the confidential funds 
were nonetheless released to the OVP on December 20, 2022, under this documented approval 
process; that the COA decision is far from being final; that there is a pending motion for 
reconsideration of that decision; that, assuming argMent/o, the COA denies that MR today, or while 
this trial is ongoing, as may plausibly happen under the circumstances, the respondent accused 
still has legal remedies available imder the law and rules, and can file a petition to question the 
validity of any forthcoming resolution of the COA on the MR under Rule 64, via Petition for 
Certiorari under Rule 65 of the Rules of Court; and, more importantly, that nothing in the Notice 
of Disallowance or COA decision states any findings of supposed misuse of confidential funds, 
as confirmed by Atty. Gloria Camora when she was asked on this point during the committee 
hearing on April 14, 2026.

We are not strangers to all these accusations, precisely because this was not the first time 
these narratives were foisted into the minds of the general public. We have heard the same tales 
and stories of these accusations since 2024, when the House conducted its Quad-Comm hearings 
and, most recently, the mini-trial of its Committee on Justice. But what we must not forget, amid 
all these accusations is the fundamental legal principle that the burden of proof never shifts—the 
burden of proof never shifts. He who accuses must prove his allegations with evidence that meets 
not only the degree of proof required and also meets the evidentiary standards of admissibility, 
credibility, relevance, materiality, and competence.

Thus, if we are to uphold the rule of law, serve justice, and invoke the accountability mechanism, then this Court— and we as a people—must guarantee that all efforts to hold our leaders accountable must be done right. This we must not forget.

blocks.normalized_text: 3 on the prosecution s proposal that more than one counsel be allowed to examine witnesses the presiding officer referred to rule xv of the rules of procedure on impeachment trials which provides that a witness shall be examined by one person on behalf of the party presenting the witness and cross-examined by one person on the opposing side accordingly he ruled that only one counsel shall conduct the direct examination or cross-examination of a witness he added however that in exceptional circumstances and when required by the orderly administration of justice the court may permit more than one counsel to examine or propound questions to a witness by way of illustration he stated that if circumstances required a public prosecutor who disagrees with a private prosecutor may take over the examination of a witness and the court could allow such substitution as an exception to the general rule he emphasized that similar exigencies would likewise be considered whenever necessary to ensure the orderly conduct of the proceedings
blocks.raw_text: 3. On the prosecution’s proposal that more than one counsel be allowed to examine witnesses, the Presiding Officer referred to Rule XV of the Rules of Procedure on Impeachment Trials, which provides that a witness shall be examined by one person on behalf of the party presenting the witness and cross-examined by one person on the opposing side. Accordingly, he ruled that only one counsel shall conduct the direct examination or cross-examination of a witness. He added, however, that in exceptional circumstances and when required by the orderly administration of justice, the Court may permit more than one counsel to examine or propound questions to a witness. By way of illustration, he stated that if circumstances required a public prosecutor who disagrees with a private prosecutor may take over the examination of a witness, and the Court could allow such substitution as an exception to the general rule. He emphasized that similar exigencies would likewise be considered whenever necessary to ensure the orderly conduct of the proceedings.
blocks.section_title: RULING OF THE PRESIDING OFFICER ON PRE-TRIAL MATTERS
blocks.source_text:
3. On the prosecution’s proposal that more than one counsel be allowed to examine 
witnesses, the Presiding Officer referred to Rule XV of the Rules of Procedure on 
Impeachment Trials, which provides that a witness shall be examined by one person 
on behalf of the party presenting the witness and cross-examined by one person on the 
opposing side. Accordingly, he ruled that only one counsel shall conduct the direct 
examination or cross-examination of a witness. He added, however, that in exceptional 
circumstances and when required by the orderly administration of justice, the Court 
may permit more than one counsel to examine or propound questions to a witness. By 
way of illustration, he stated that if circumstances required a public prosecutor who 
disagrees with a private prosecutor may take over the examination of a witness, and 
the Court could allow such substitution as an exception to the general rule. He 
emphasized that similar exigencies would likewise be considered whenever necessary 
to ensure the orderly conduct of the proceedings.

4. With respect to the sealed Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) box transmitted to the 
Senate as part of the documentary materials accompanying the Articles of Impeachment, 
the Presiding Officer stated that the Court had not yet acquired custodia legis over 
the box, as it had neither been ordered produced by the Court nor formally offered and 
marked in evidence. Accordingly, he directed that the sealed box be returned to the 
Bureau of Internal Revenue, without prejudice to any subsequent motion by either party 
for the issuance of the appropriate subpoena, which the Court would resolve at the 
proper time.

At the direction of Presiding Officer Escudero, the Clerk of Court proceeded to read into the 
record the Prosecution’s request for the issuance of subpoena duces tecum for bank records and 
Anti-Money Laundering Council (AMLC) records dated June 18, 2026, to wit:
Request for issuance of subpoena duces tecum for bank records
“The prosecution respectfully requests the issuance of a subpoena duces tecum to the branch 
manager of the following banks: BPI-Julia Vargas branch, BPI-Greenhills EDSA branch, Philippine 
Savings Bank-Wilson branch, BDO Unibank Inc.-Davao CM Recto branch, Metrobank-Davao 
Ecoland branch, BDO Unibank-Davao Rizal branch. Security Bank-Bicutan branch, LandBank-San 
Pedro branch, Philippine Savings Bank-Davao MADR branch, requiring him or her, or his or her 
authorized representative, to submit to the Clerk of the Impeachment Court, on or before 24 June 
2026 at 9:00 a.m., the certified true copies of all records, documents, books, papers, electronic data, 
communications, and information in the possession, custody, or control of the bank relating to any 
account—deposit, investment, trust, loan, safety deposit, or other financial transactions— 
maintained, opened, closed, controlled, beneficially owned, or jointly held by Sara Z. Duterte, and/ 
or Manases R. Carpio, whether individually, jointly with each other, jointly with any other person, 
or through any authorized representative, nominee, attomey-in-fact, or signatory, for the period 
January 1, 2007 to December 31, 2025, including, but not limited to:
(1) Records identifying account numbers, account types, know your customer documents, such as, 
but not limited to, customer information forms and signature cards;
(2) Monthly bank statements, average daily balances per calendar year, year-end balances from 
January 1, 2007 to December 31, 2025;
(3) All account and transaction records, ledgers and journals and similar instruments fi-om January 
1, 2007 to December 31, 2025; and
(4) All other relevant bank documents.”

Attorney Sison requested that the Defense be furnished copies of both the subpoena 
request filed in June 2026, and the refiled request. She manifested, however, that the subpoena 
appeared to cover documents from 2006 to 2025, which she noted was inconsistent with the 
ruling in Duterte vs. HOR that states that the impeachment must be limited to the acts or omissions 
within the impeachable years and terms of office of the official. Nonetheless, she requested 10 days 
to file the Defense’s comment pertaining to Article U, noting that the Prosecution intended 
to proceed first with Article IV.

Presiding Officer Escudero directed that all motions for the issuance of subpoenas 
ad testificandum and subpoenas duces tecum filed by either party be furnished to the opposing 
party prior to filing before the Impeachment Court, to provide full disclosure and fairness. 
He further granted both parties five days within which to submit their respective comments, 
memoranda, or positions papers on the subpoena requests and stated that the parties would be 
given an opportunity to present oral arguments before the senator-judges prior to the Court’s 
resolution of the matter.

period would be reckoned from that day, with filing permitted on the following Monday since the

later date with due notice to the parties.

blocks.raw_text: Senator-Judge Cayetano (A), citing the earlier statement of the Court that it would adopt the literal interpretation of Article XI, Section 3 (6) of the Constitution that no person shall be convicted without the concurrence of two-thirds of all the members of the Senate, he proposed that, at the appropriate time, the Impeachment Court consider coordinating with the Sandiganbayan to facilitate the attendance of Senator-Judges Marcoleta and Estrada during the trial. He noted that, should the proceedings reach the presentation of evidence, their presence would enable them to observe the witnesses and fully participate as Senator-Judges. He argued that the Impeachment Court is sui generis and cited the Senate’s practice of coordinating with courts for the temporary attendance of persons under judicial custody. Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) further suggested that be taken up in a caucus, an in-camera discussion, or the submission of legal opinions, and that the parties may also be invited to comment. blocks.source_text:
Senator-Judge Cayetano (A), citing the earlier statement of the Court that it would adopt the 
literal interpretation of Article XI, Section 3 (6) of the Constitution that no person shall be convicted 
without the concurrence of two-thirds of all the members of the Senate, he proposed that, at the 
appropriate time, the Impeachment Court consider coordinating with the Sandiganbayan to facilitate 
the attendance of Senator-Judges Marcoleta and Estrada during the trial. He noted that, should the 
proceedings reach the presentation of evidence, their presence would enable them to observe the 
witnesses and fully participate as Senator-Judges. He argued that the Impeachment Court is sui 
generis and cited the Senate’s practice of coordinating with courts for the temporary attendance 
of persons under judicial custody. Senator-Judge Cayetano (A) further suggested that be taken up 
in a caucus, an in-camera discussion, or the submission of legal opinions, and that the parties may 
also be invited to comment. He expressed the view that the absence of the two Senator-Judges 
could affect the voting threshold required for conviction and emphasized the importance of ensuring 
a fair impeachment trial.

Lauding the Impeachment Court’s initiative to make the records of the proceedings publicly 
accessible, Senator-Judge Aquino inquired when the uploading of documentary evidence would 
be completed. The Presiding Officer stated that the records are voluminous and that most of 
the marked exhibits are also part of the Articles of Impeachment and their Annexes, which had

presented evidence at the soonest possible time except for the Journal, which could only be 
uploaded after the impeachment court’s approval.

blocks.raw_text: The Presiding Officer clarified that the scheduled date for deliberation only concerns the issuance of a subpoena duces tecum and ad testificandum for AMLC records, not the actual records. Should the subpoena be granted, he said that the documents would still have to be presented by the parties. blocks.source_text:
The Presiding Officer clarified that the scheduled date for deliberation only concerns the 
issuance of a subpoena duces tecum and ad testificandum for AMLC records, not the actual 
records. Should the subpoena be granted, he said that the documents would still have to be 
presented by the parties. He clarified that the impeachment court could not discuss AMLC records 
in Executive session as matters taken up therein could not be revealed to the public nor used in 
the proceedings. He further said that limiting the discussion of the evidence in executive session 
would effectively hogtie that piece of evidence. He said that the issues on whether the documents 
could be made public or could be legally obtained would be decided by the impeachment court 
after hearing the parties’ oral arguments.
